idioms.definition: To lose an opportunity by being too slow or late to act
examples: If you don't apply today, you'll miss the boat – the deadline is tonight.
He missed the boat on investing in that company years ago.
Don't miss the boat – this offer is only available this week.
idioms.usageTip: Often used to express regret about a lost opportunity or to warn someone to act quickly. The opportunity is gone or nearly gone.

idioms.idiom: at the drop of a hat
idioms.definition: Immediately; without hesitation or preparation; whenever asked
examples: She's always ready to help – she'd do it at the drop of a hat.
He can give a presentation at the drop of a hat.
I would move abroad at the drop of a hat if the right job came along.
idioms.usageTip: Emphasises instant willingness or readiness. Often used to describe someone who is very flexible or highly enthusiastic about something.

idioms.definition: To fail to reach a required or expected standard or level
examples: The team's performance fell short of expectations.
Sales figures fell short of the target by 15%.
Despite their best efforts, the project fell short.
idioms.usageTip: Often followed by 'of': 'fall short of something'. Used in business, sports, and everyday contexts to describe underperformance.

idioms.definition: Just in time; at the last possible moment before it is too late
examples: The ambulance arrived in the nick of time.
She submitted the application in the nick of time.
He caught the falling vase in the nick of time.
idioms.usageTip: Has a dramatic or exciting quality – often used in stories or to emphasise how close a situation came to going wrong.

idioms.definition: To change your opinion, decision, or feelings about something
examples: She had a change of heart and decided to stay in her current job.
I wasn't going to attend, but I had a change of heart at the last minute.
He had a change of heart about selling the house.
idioms.usageTip: Suggests an emotional or personal shift in attitude. Often used when someone unexpectedly reverses a decision.

idioms.definition: To set a limit on what you will accept or allow; to decide that something is unacceptable
examples: I don't mind working late, but I draw the line at working weekends.
You have to draw the line somewhere – you can't say yes to everything.
She drew the line when her boss asked her to falsify the report.
idioms.usageTip: Often followed by 'at': 'draw the line at something'. Used to define a personal or moral boundary.

fillInExercises.sentence: You'd better book your tickets now or you'll ___. The event always sells out.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Miss the boat' means to lose an opportunity because you were too slow to act.

fillInExercises.sentence: He'd quit his job and move to another country ___.
options: at the drop of a hat
fillInExercises.correct: at the drop of a hat
fillInExercises.explanation: 'At the drop of a hat' means immediately or without hesitation.

fillInExercises.sentence: The fundraising campaign ___ – they raised £40,000 but needed £50,000.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fall short' means to not quite reach the required or expected level.

fillInExercises.sentence: The fire brigade arrived ___ and saved most of the building.
options: at the drop of a hat
fillInExercises.explanation: 'In the nick of time' means just in time, at the last possible moment.

fillInExercises.sentence: She was going to resign, but ___ and decided to try a different department first.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Have a change of heart' means to reverse a decision or change your feelings about something.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'll work overtime when needed, but I ___ at being asked to skip my holiday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Draw the line' means to set a personal limit on what you will accept or tolerate.

lines.text: Did you hear? Elena decided not to take the promotion after all.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Really? She [had a change of heart|have a change of heart]? She seemed so excited about it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: 'Have a change of heart' means to reverse a decision or change your feelings about something.

explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Really? She had a change of heart? She seemed so excited about it.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *have a change of heart* (*have a change of heart*).

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: lose an opportunity by acting too late

pairs.idiom: at the drop of a hat

pairs.meaning: fail to reach the required level or standard
set a personal limit on what you accept

quizExercises.sentence: The results ___ of what the investors were expecting, which caused some concern.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Fall short' means to fail to meet the expected standard or target.

quizExercises.sentence: She rescued the documents ___ – just minutes before the system crashed.
options: at the drop of a hat
quizExercises.explanation: 'In the nick of time' means at the very last moment before disaster or too late.

quizExercises.question: Which situation best illustrates 'having a change of heart'?
options: You plan to sell your car, but then decide to keep it after all
You have always loved your job and continue to love it
You finish a task and immediately start the next one
quizExercises.explanation: 'Have a change of heart' means reversing a decision or changing your feelings about something.

quizExercises.question: When do you 'draw the line'?
options: When you are finishing a drawing or diagram
When you decide you will not accept or tolerate something beyond a certain point
When you need to define a deadline for a project
quizExercises.explanation: 'Draw the line' means to set a personal or moral limit beyond which you will not go.
